critique.summary: The author describes their transition from extensively editing digital photos in Adobe Lightroom to embracing film photography and digital cameras that produce high-quality JPEGs with minimal editing. They highlight their experience with analogue film and new digital cameras that reduce the need for post-processing, leading to the cancellation of their Adobe subscription.
mainPoints: The author used Adobe Lightroom for many years to edit RAW digital photos.
In 2022, they started using an analogue film camera (Leica M6), which introduced them to easier editing workflows via lab scans.
They became frustrated with the cost of Adobe's subscription and stopped editing photos extensively.
They now use digital cameras like the Camp Snap Pro and Fujifilm X100VI that provide better JPEGs straight from the camera, reducing the need for editing.
The author enjoys focusing on taking photos rather than editing them on a computer.
critique.truthfulness: The claims made in the article are consistent with common experiences among photographers who shift from digital RAW editing to film or high-quality JPEG workflows. The author accurately describes the cost of Adobe Lightroom subscriptions and the typical workflow differences between RAW editing and film scanning. The mention of specific camera models and their capabilities aligns with publicly available information about those products. However, the article is a personal account and does not cite external sources or provide multiple perspectives.
considerations: The article does not discuss the potential limitations of relying solely on JPEGs, such as reduced flexibility in post-processing compared to RAW files.
There is no mention of alternative software options for photo editing that might be less costly than Adobe Lightroom.
The environmental impact or cost considerations of using film photography versus digital are not addressed.
The article does not explore the quality differences between various film labs or scanning processes.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on photographers' varied approaches to photo editing, with some embracing minimal editing or straight-out-of-camera shots to preserve spontaneity, while others value detailed post-processing for creative control and print quality. Many express frustration with subscription-based software and advocate for open-source alternatives.

comments.author: chromacity
comments.text:
I don't want to dunk on people who are discovering the charms of retro tech, but as someone who started with film and spent a fair amount of time in the darkroom, I was delighted to discover the hassle-free simplicity and dependability of digital photography, so it is a bit mind-boggling that people want to go back to that for casual use cases.

It reminds me of people buying vinyl, using VHS filters on social media, etc. I think it's more about signaling some cultural identity than any objective benefits of the "retro" process. It's not like digital cameras make you give up creative control. If you want to limit yourself to 36 unreviewed shots, you can do that with digital too.

That said, I agree with one thing: you shouldn't be paying for an Adobe subscription. Use Darktable, Capture One, or some other equivalent that you're not just renting for life.
comments.analysis: Provides a balanced perspective based on experience, correctly identifies cultural trends and practical software alternatives.

comments.author: wesleyd
comments.text:
I admire what this person is doing, but some reasons I prefer raw + lightroom over eg camera jpeg are:

* Lightroom’s noise reduction is WAY better than what my camera (a D500) can do. I shoot sports, usually indoors, with highish iso, so NR’s gonna have to happen at some point.

* If I’m going to lug around a dedicated camera, I’m gonna have it do its best. I have my iPhone for everything else.

* I can apply today’s lightroom NR to raws I shot years ago. Similarly, I expect to be able to apply future lightroom’s NR to today’s raws.

* Lightroom Classic is a superb program - it has many warts and clunks and oddities but it achieved product market fit and it stayed there, doing what its users want. Adobe keep making small improvements, and yet they don’t fuck it up!! This is vanishingly rare in big tech!!! (Promos gonna promo!) I grudgingly pay for this.

(My theory as to how they have managed to resist the institutional imperative to destroy Lightroom classic is that they created a fork, named just “Lightroom”, on which the promo can wreak its destruction, it’s kind of a second golgafrinchan ark, leaving Lightroom classic alone. I pay for Lightroom classic as a way of saying: keep leaving it alone!)
comments.analysis: Insightful explanation of technical benefits of raw editing and software longevity; acknowledges trade-offs realistically.

comments.author: asow92
comments.text:
I've come to the same realization after shooting digital, film, and back to digital again.

I've found that if I apply "recipes" or "presets" to my camera and shoot jpg I get roughly what I want straight out of camera. In fact, I find that shooting jpg exclusively with a preset _almost_ scratches that film itch: there is a kind of permanency to the rendered output, and that forces me to slow down and think about what I want to render with this subject like one does with film.

Once I'm done shooting I simply import to Apple photos and make very light edits from there if any before sharing.

It's liberating to embrace constraints and reduce tooling. You might even have fun.
comments.analysis: Offers a practical workflow that balances creative constraints with minimal editing, reflecting a nuanced understanding.

comments.author: CarVac
comments.text:
I got tired of in depth fiddly editing and wrote Filmulator to minimize the decision-making and streamline editing. I rarely spend more than 20-30 seconds per image.

You get a clean, basic look, no weird colors or overly creative "looks", but with adjustability and great highlight handling that JPEG doesn't get you.

https://filmulator.org

The current builds there are quite old but we've got new ones coming.
comments.analysis: Presents a concrete solution addressing common editing frustrations, demonstrating technical initiative.

comments.author: anta40
comments.text:
Whatever floats the boat.

Even back to film/analog era, taking a photo is just the 1st step. Then apply some darkroom work (dodge/burn/use some filters to adjust the highlight/shadow etc etc). Image editing softwares like Photoshop simplify the process.

I mostly shoot in black & white (both film and digital). Since once of my biggest inspirations is Ansel Adams, then no I don't adhere to "SOOC" (straight out of camera) philosophy. Fine tuning in Photoshop is a must.
comments.analysis: Accurately contextualizes editing as integral to photography history and artistic expression.

comments.author: arvinsim
comments.text:
My process is to take a lot of photos, then ruthlessly cull them before I do any editing.

I usually keep around 10% of the total photos for editing. After that, I do another round of culling and keep only the best.

I also follow a philosophy of "good enough". If left to my own devices, I would probably endlessly edit photos.

I edit a single photo for around 3 minutes. That way, I will not feel stuck.
comments.analysis: Describes an efficient workflow balancing quality and time management, a practical approach.

comments.author: lizknope
comments.text:
Do whatever you want that makes you happy.

I take thousands of photos a year with my phone and less than 1% of them get edited.

I take thousands of photos with my Nikon in RAW / NEF format. I have over 50 large photos printed in my house and editing absolutely helps when you print 20x30" or higher.
comments.analysis: Highlights the importance of editing for print quality, while acknowledging casual use cases.

comments.text: Never been a fan of editing photos, especially as I have difficulties dealing with the infinite possibilities. I just stick with default output from the camera and just fix backlight or some minor adjustments required. I'm a big fan of Foveon sensor (sigma dp, sigma sd) and lately fp and BF, it helped a lot as they really know color and I love how they deal with it. I also shot and process black and white film, and appreciate the "deal with film/developper characteristics" approach too.
comments.analysis: Shares personal preferences that align with known sensor characteristics and film aesthetics.

comments.author: sanitycheck
comments.text:
I'll be sticking with Lightroom 6 (non-subscription) and the old cameras it supports, until the sad but inevitable day I can no longer run it.

I don't find editing takes much time, because I now have so many custom presets I can apply on import or in bulk that do 90% of the work.

What does take ages is picking out the best shots, but really the only way to make that quicker is to take fewer photos. Which I suppose shooting film actually does force you to do. (But so would a 2GB SD card.)
comments.analysis: Provides a realistic view on workflow bottlenecks and benefits of presets and shooting constraints.

comments.author: exoro
comments.text: If the straw that broke the camel's back is the nagging Adobe subscription, why not just learn Darktable or Raw Therappee?
comments.analysis: Offers practical advice on alternative software, relevant to the discussion.

comments.author: bix6
comments.text: I’m almost done moving from Lightroom to Darktable. Lightroom is amazing but I don’t edit enough these days to justify $20/mo.
comments.analysis: Reflects a common user experience balancing software cost and usage.

comments.author: kvgr
comments.text:
I started photography last year, i shoot raw because i dont like sony colors. But I have very quick process: "auto", little fidgeting with sliders, one in 10 photos gets a mask for sky and then i apply some preset that i like most for the photo. I just cant spend 30minutes on one photo.

But the editing process is very subjective. even in era of film there was a lot of processing, colors with chemicals, fixing defects. Just manual photoshop.

I understand the simplicity and joy of purists, but to each his own i guess.
comments.analysis: Correctly notes that editing complexity is not unique to digital and shares a pragmatic workflow.

comments.author: rpgbr
comments.text: I mostly try Apple Photos’ “magic” editing. It’s hit and miss, but when it hits, the photo gets way better. When not, I adjust a couple sliders (contrast, brightness, saturation). In both cases, only when I’ll use the photo. Otherwise, editing tools will be there for when (and if) I need them.
comments.analysis: Describes a flexible and low-effort editing approach suitable for casual photographers.

comments.author: Retr0id
comments.text: I set my camera to save both JPEG and RAW. 95% of the time the camera's JPEG is fine so I just use that (maybe with some final adjustments in GIMP), but it's nice to have the RAW around in case more significant edits are needed.
comments.analysis: Represents a common and sensible workflow balancing convenience and flexibility.

comments.author: rr808
comments.text: Me too, I figured I spent more time on the computer than taking pics. Now I shoot jpg and if I have some spare time I go out and shoot. If I take a good pic I share it with basic editing if any instead of waiting to get it "perfect".
comments.analysis: Emphasizes enjoying photography and sharing over perfectionism, a valid personal philosophy.

comments.author: jmclnx
comments.analysis: Provides useful technical advice for privacy and copyright management.

comments.author: zecg
comments.text: This FLOSS RAW editor works really well, btw: http://www.rawtherapee.com/
comments.analysis: Valid recommendation supporting alternatives to commercial software.

comments.author: rickdg
comments.text: I just use Pixelmator Pro for a quick workflow. There's a nice feedback loop between taking the shot and editing it later.
comments.analysis: Shares a practical tool choice for efficient editing.
